channel.title: CyberKareem security writing
channel.description: Security research, engineering notes, certification reviews, and authorized-lab walkthroughs by Abdullah Kareem.

item.title: CVE-2026-55675: When a Fork PR Crosses into a Privileged Workflow
item.description: How untrusted pull-request code reached a secret-bearing CI job—and how separating build from deploy closed the trust-boundary failure.

item.title: CVE-2026-55890: The Sibling Sink an Incomplete Fix Missed
item.description: A Grav case study in variant analysis: one Markdown media-attribute sink was blocked while a sibling style path remained writable.

item.title: CVE-2026-59185: An Installation ID Is Not Proof of Ownership
item.description: How a client-supplied GitHub App installation ID crossed a tenant boundary, and why OAuth identity must bind the installation to the workspace.

item.title: CVE-2026-59889: A Missing JsonView Gate in JsonUnwrapped Deserialization
item.description: Why Jackson’s unwrapped-property replay bypassed a write-side JsonView boundary, enabling authorization-sensitive fields to be populated.

item.title: CVE-2026-59979: Authentication Is Not Tenant Authorization
item.description: How authentication-only routes and globally keyed service lookups enabled cross-tenant reads and state changes in Yosemite-Crew.

item.title: CVE-2026-61448: How Malformed Content-Type Reached Stored XSS
item.description: A Parse Server upload-validation case study: malformed media types survived storage and let browsers reinterpret active content.

item.title: From Source VLAN to Evidence Bundle: Building a PCI DSS Segmentation Test Runner
item.description: A practical look at automating bounded reachability tests from authorized source segments toward explicit CDE targets while keeping methodology, coverage, and compliance decisions human.

item.title: HackTheBox “CrossFit” Walkthrough
item.description: Hack The Box — CrossFit Walkthrough Comprehensive Step-by-Step Guide with Hacker Mindset

item.title: HackTheBox “CodePartTwo” Walkthrough
item.description: CodePartTwo is an Easy Linux machine that features a vulnerable Flask-based web application. Initial web enumeration reveals a JavaScript code editor powered by a vulnerable version of js2py, which allows for remote…

item.title: HackTheBox “Celestial” Walkthrough
item.description: Celestial is a medium difficulty machine which focuses on deserialization exploits. It is not the most realistic, however it provides a practical example of abusing client-side serialized objects in NodeJS framework.

item.title: HackTheBox “Cache” Walkthrough
item.description: A Medium Linux box that teaches why client-side authentication, unauthenticated caches, and docker group membership are a recipe for full compromise.

item.title: HackTheBox “CTF” Walkthrough
item.description: CTF is an insane difficulty Linux box with a web application using LDAP based authentication. The application is vulnerable to LDAP injection but due to character blacklisting the payloads need to be double URL…

item.title: HackTheBox “Book” Walkthrough
item.description: A Medium Linux box where three small logic flaws, an admin takeover, a PDF renderer betrayal, and a symlink race, chain into full system compromise.

item.title: HackTheBox “Bolt” Walkthrough
item.description: Before touching a single tool, understand the goal: enumerate everything, trust nothing, and follow every thread. Every service is a potential entry point. Every file is a potential credential store. Every…

item.title: HackTheBox “Atlas” Walkthrough
item.description: Some boxes hand you a foothold and ask you to climb. Atlas gives you the blueprints, lets you read the architect’s notes, and then asks whether you understand how the building was put together. This is a Hard Windows…

item.title: HackTheBox “Arkham” Walkthrough
item.description: Some machines teach you a single technique. Others force you to connect the dots across multiple domains networking, cryptography, web exploitation, and Windows internals. Arkham is one of those boxes.

item.title: Hack The Box: Sizzle Walkthrough
item.description: An archived walkthrough of the retired Hack The Box Sizzle machine, preserved from Abdullah Kareem's OSEP Prep preparation notes.

item.title: Secure Automated Purge: Building a Bootable Disk-Sanitization Utility
item.description: An archived engineering account of a bootable disk-sanitization prototype that requires device-specific validation and review against NIST SP 800-88 Rev.2 before operational use.

item.title: Stoicism in Everyday Life: How Ancient Wisdom (and a Few Modern Insights) Transformed My Anxious Mind
item.description: This reflection shares my personal journey with Stoicism and mental resilience, highlighting lessons learned that have positively impacted both my personal and professional life.

item.title: Introducing the BitLocker Cryptographic Erase Utility: Secure Data Destruction Made Simple
item.description: When it comes time to retire, repurpose, or sell a computer, one challenge looms large: how do you make sure no one can recover your data? Simply deleting files or doing a quick format isn’t enough — the files often…

item.title: HackTheBox “Tabby” Walkthrough
item.description: Tabby, an easy-level Windows OS machine on HackTheBox, offers a journey from LFI discovery to root access. Beginning with finding an LFI vulnerability, we obtain Tomcat server credentials, leading to shell access via…

item.title: HackTheBox “Access” Walkthrough
item.description: Access, an easy-level Windows OS machine on HackTheBox, encountered unexpected hurdles like FTP stability issues. Key breakthroughs included discovering anonymous FTP access, obtaining credentials, and progressing to…

item.title: HackTheBox “Forest” Walkthrough
item.description: Forest, an easy-level Windows OS machine on HackTheBox, offers an opportunity to explore Active Directory exploitation. Through RPC enumeration and Impacket’s GetNPUsers.py, we obtain credentials and access as…

item.title: HackTheBox “Cap” Walkthrough
item.description: Cap, an easy-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, it starts with the discovery of clear-text credentials hidden in a PCAP file for initial access. The exploitation then targets Python with the cap_setuid capability…

item.title: HackTheBox “Active” Walkthrough
item.description: Active, an easy-level Windows OS machine on HackTheBox, started by discovering an open SMB share, initiating a journey through various stages of exploitation. Unveiling a Group Policy Preference password within the…

item.title: HackTheBox “GoodGames” Walkthrough
item.description: GoodGames, an easy-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, the journey begins with a glaring SQL injection flaw, offering us a path to circumvent login authentication and execute union injections for data extraction.…

item.title: HackTheBox “Remote” Walkthrough
item.description: Remote, an easy-level Windows OS machine on HackTheBox, the journey unfolds with the hunt for a crucial hash hidden within a config file accessible via NFS. Cracking this hash becomes the key to unlocking the…

item.title: HackTheBox “BountyHunter” Walkthrough
item.description: BountyHunter, an easy-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, we will discover the intricacies of a straightforward yet potent XXE vulnerability nestled within a webpage, offering a gateway to accessing crucial files…

item.title: Microsoft Teams: Classic vs. New — Features and Security Unveiled
item.description: Microsoft Teams is one of the most popular collaboration platforms in the world, with over 250 million monthly active users. Teams helps you chat, call, meet, and work with your colleagues, partners, and customers in…

item.title: HackTheBox “Luanne” Walkthrough
item.description: Luanne, an easy-level Unix “NetBSD” OS machine on HackTheBox, through the Supervisor Process Manager, exploiting a command injection vulnerability in a Lua-backed API on port 80. That granted me access to a localhost…

item.title: HackTheBox “Passage” Walkthrough
item.description: Passage, a medium-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, features a CuteNews web application susceptible to a remote command execution vulnerability. Exploiting this flaw provides initial access. After cracking a…

item.title: HackTheBox “Doctor” Walkthrough
item.description: Doctor, an easy-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, a platform resembling a digital message board. The challenge involves seeking out a vulnerability tied to “command injection” in order to attain a coveted shell.…

item.title: HackTheBox “Magic” Walkthrough
item.description: Magic, a medium-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, commences by employing a SQL Injection technique to circumvent the login mechanism. Subsequently, a web-shell is uploaded in a form of an image file to evade…

item.title: HackTheBox “OpenAdmin” Walkthrough
item.description: OpenAdmin, an easy-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, involves conducting some enumeration to uncover an instance of OpenNetAdmin. Leveraging a remote code execution exploit within OpenNetAdmin, I successfully…

item.title: HackTheBox “Traverxec” Walkthrough
item.description: Traverxec, an easy-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, necessitating thorough enumeration and exploitation of the web server, Nostromo. The initial step involved capitalizing on a Remote Code Execution (RCE)…

item.title: HackTheBox “Mango” Walkthrough
item.description: Mango, a medium-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, centers around the exploitation of a NoSQL document database to circumvent an authorization page and subsequently expose database-related details. Upon obtaining…

item.title: HackTheBox “Blocky” Walkthrough
item.description: Blocky, an easy-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, it definitely needed some patience while enumeration. Can you believe there were these sneaky Java Jar files hidden away in the /plugins path? Well, luckily, I…

item.title: HackTheBox “Popcorn” Walkthrough
item.description: Popcorn, a medium-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, through meticulous enumeration, we stumbled upon an instance of TorrentHoster, which allowed us to upload an image which is actually a reverse shell payload and…

item.title: HackTheBox “Mirai” Walkthrough
item.description: Mirai, an easy-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, runs on RaspberryPi device and has Pi-Hole application installed. The default username and password for the device are still active via SSH. The user has sudo…

item.title: HackTheBox “Jarvis” Walkthrough
item.description: Jarvis, a medium-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, entails leveraging a SQL injection vulnerability to establish initial access, capitalizing on a Python script for privilege escalation to the “pepper” user, and…

item.title: HackTheBox “SwagShop” Walkthrough
item.description: SwagShop, an easy-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, it presents a Magento eCommerce interface. The process to obtain a shell involves the utilization of two exploits. Firstly, an authentication bypass exploit is…

item.title: HackTheBox “Networked” Walkthrough
item.description: Networked, an easy-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, it entailed the exploitation of a susceptible file upload functionality to establish initial entry, followed by the utilization of multiple scripts running as…

item.title: HackTheBox “FriendZone” Walkthrough
item.description: FriendZone, an easy-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, through the use of zone transfer technique, the discovery of virtual hosts is facilitated. Subsequently, accessible shares on the Samba service yield valuable…

item.title: HackTheBox “Irked” Walkthrough
item.description: Irked, an easy-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, encompassing the exploitation of a specific vulnerability to enable remote file download and execution for initial access. The subsequent escalation of privileges…

item.title: HackTheBox “Sunday” Walkthrough
item.description: Sunday, an easy-level Solaris OS machine on HackTheBox, involves performing user enumeration on the Finger service to discover the sunny user. Afterward, a brute-force attack using Hydra is employed to authenticate…

item.title: HackTheBox “Conceal” Walkthrough
item.description: Conceal, a hard-level Windows OS machine on HackTheBox, utilizes IPSec for secure server connectivity. After performing enumerating SNMP, we discovered a preshared key, which helped us to establish a connection to…

item.title: HackTheBox “Poison” Walkthrough
item.description: Poison, a medium-level FreeBSD OS machine on HackTheBox, features a vulnerable web service that is susceptible to a local file inclusion vulnerability. By exploiting this vulnerability, an obfuscated password…

item.title: HackTheBox “Jerry” Walkthrough
item.description: Jerry, an easy-level Windows OS machine on HackTheBox, presented a favorable level of difficulty. The initial task involved leveraging default credentials to gain access to the Tomcat Web Application Manager. By…

item.title: HackTheBox “Valentine” Walkthrough
item.description: Valentine, an easy-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, a vulnerable web service affected by Heartbleed exploit. By leveraging this vulnerability, an attacker extracts a password from memory Combined with an…

item.title: HackTheBox “Bounty” Walkthrough
item.description: Bounty, an easy-level Windows OS machine on HackTheBox, a straightforward Windows challenge, where the objective was to exploit a Windows ASP web server by uploading a web.config file. This allowed for remote code…

item.title: HackTheBox “Node” Walkthrough
item.description: Node, a medium-level Linux OS machine on HackTheBox, focuses on the meticulous enumeration of a NodeJS application to identify an API endpoint that exposes user password hashes. This challenge presents an opportunity…

item.title: HackTheBox “Silo” Walkthrough
item.description: Silo, a medium-level Windows OS machine on HackTheBox, we will take advantage of a vulnerability in its Oracle database to infiltrate the system. To elevate our privileges, we will examine a dumped memory file from…

item.title: HackTheBox “Grandpa” Walkthrough
item.description: Grandpa is an easy Windows Hack The Box machine centered on an IIS 6 WebDAV buffer-overflow path and subsequent privilege escalation.

item.title: HackTheBox “Sense” Walkthrough
item.description: Sense, an easy-level FreeBSD OS machine on HackTheBox, revolves around identifying user credentials for a pfSense Firewall machine, a task that forms the foundation of this endeavor. Moreover, exploiting a well-known…

item.title: HackTheBox “Arctic” Walkthrough
item.description: Arctic, an easy-level Windows machine on HackTheBox, presents a straightforward challenge in which an arbitrary file upload vulnerability in the Adobe ColdFusion 8 web application was exploited. By leveraging this…

item.title: HackTheBox “Cronos” Walkthrough
item.description: Cronos, a medium-level Linux machine on HackTheBox, presents an opportunity to demonstrate exploiting security weaknesses. The initial stage involved leveraging an SQL injection vulnerability to gain unauthorized…

item.title: HackTheBox “Granny” Walkthrough
item.description: The Machine Granny is a straightforward Windows-based challenge on the HackTheBox platform. The objective of this challenge revolves around leveraging the PUT method within the WebDAV extension of the HTTP protocol…

item.title: HackTheBox “Nibbles” Walkthrough
item.description: Nibbles is a beginner-friendly Linux machine on the Hack The Box platform. The exploit path involves exploiting an arbitrary file upload vulnerability in the My Image plugin of the Nibbleblog web application. This…

item.title: HackTheBox “Bastard” Walkthrough
item.description: In this walkthrough, we delve into the HackTheBox machine named “Bastard.” By exploiting the Drupal 7 vulnerability (CVE-2018–7600), we gain command execution. Afterwards, we establish a reverse shell and showcase…

item.title: HackTheBox “Bashed” With & Without Metasploit WriteUp
item.description: Bashed is a retired machine available on the HackTheBox platform, renowned for its ease of completion, which aligns with its official rating. The journey commences with the discovery of a hidden web shell.

item.title: HackTheBox “Devel” With & Without Metasploit WriteUp
item.description: The Devel machine on Hack The Box is a Windows host that permits anonymous login to its FTP service. This vulnerability can be exploited by uploading files onto the server and establishing a reverse shell on the…

item.title: HackTheBox “Shocker” With & Without Metasploit WriteUp
item.description: The Shocker is a machine on Hack The Box that is based on a vulnerability named Shellshock which is found in Bash Shell. This vulnerability can be found on most of the Linux and Unix servers and network devices. The…

item.title: Offensive Security Wireless Professional (OSWP) Certification Review
item.description: I just got my results and want to share my experience with the course and exam for Offensive Security Wireless Attacks PEN-210.

item.title: 90 Days of My Experience With Personal Journaling
item.description: Whether you keep a physical or digital journal, sitting and recording your thoughts is a great way to externalize your feelings. It’s beautiful to look back on entries from years past and remember a happy event, and…

item.title: eCPPT: eLearnSecurity Certified Professional Penetration Tester — Review
item.description: I completed my eCPPTv2 exam, in July 2022, and received a reply on 1st August 2022, stating that i am now an eCPPTv2.

item.title: HackTheBox “Lame” With & Without Metasploit WriteUp
item.description: Lame is a beginner level machine, requiring only one exploit to obtain root access. It was the first machine published on Hack The Box and was often the first machine for new users prior to its retirement.

item.title: Installing the Metasploit-framework On Ubuntu
item.description: To install the latest version of the Metasploit package, this one-liner will add the nightly build repository, import the Rapid7 GPG key and install the package:
